subject: otolaryngology
title: Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders
preambleMarkdown:
# Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders is a clinical condition or syndrome in otolaryngology.
- This article focuses on ear, nose, throat, airway, hearing, vestibular, sinus, and head-neck pathology.
- The practical clinical priority is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In otolaryngology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused otolaryngology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses otolaryngology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Miscellaneous ear, nose, and throat disorders draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

COM MON OTOLARYN&OLO&IC CONDITIONS
I. Acute Sinusitis
-
A. General principles
l . Infection of the paranasal sinuses for less than 4 weeks ( 4 to 12 weeks = sub
acute; > l2 weeks = chronic)
2. Most commonly results from a viral upper respiratory infection (URI)
3. Minimal mucosal inflammation can obstruct the drainage of the sinuses, lead
ing to bacterial infection of retained sinus secretions, typically during the
second week of the URI.

4. The most common bacterial pathogens are Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemoph
ilus inKuenzae, and MorBella catarrhalis.

5. Other common predisposing factors for bacterial infection include allergies,
anatomic obstruction of the nose, odontogenic infection, intranasal drug abuse,
immunodeHciency, and impaired mucociliary clearance (e.g. , cystic Hbrosis) .

6. Acute invasive fungal sinusitis may occur in immunocompromised patients or
those with poorly controlled diabetes and is a medical emergency.

a. Most common fungal organisms are Mucor, Rhizopus, and Aspergillus.

B. Clinical features
l . Nasal congestion
2. Postnasal drip and purulent nasal drainage
3. Facial pain or pressure that is worse when bending forward, maxillary tooth
pain

## 4. Headache

## 5. Hyposmia/anosmia

C. Diagnosis (see Clinical Pearl l 8- l )
l . Endoscopic evaluation: Visualize purulent drainage a s well a s septal deviation,
nasal polyps , and other obstructing features that may cause blockage of sinus
drainage.

CLIN ICAL PEARL 1 8- 1
Criteria for t h e Diagnosis o f Sinusitis*
Major Criteria M i nor Criteria
- Purulent a nterior nasal d ischarge - Headache
- Purulent posterior nasal d i scharge - Ear pain, pressure, or fu l l ness
- Nasal congestion or obstruction - H a l itosis
- Fac i a l congestion or fu l l ness - Denta l p a i n
- Hyposmia or anosmia - Fever (subacute or chronic s i n usitis)
- Fever (acute s i n usitis on ly) - Fatigue
* D iagnosis of si nusitis made i n the presence of at least two m a j o r criteria o r one m a j o r and two or more m i n o r criteria.

c Ii
2. Imaging: indicated in chronic/recurrent sinusitis or complicated cases of acute
sinusitis
a. Computed tomography (CT) scan: method of choice; can assess bony
structures as well as evaluate for air-fluid levels and mucosal thickening
b. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) : does not image bone as well as CT
but is good for differentiating soft-tissue masses from retained mucus,
particularly when extra-sinus involvement is suspected
3. Laboratory studies: HIV and immunoglobulin serologies are useful in
refractory cases of sinusitis if there is any evidence of an immunocompromised
state.

D. Treatment
l . Medical management
a. Prevention
( 1 ) Nasal saline and/or nasal steroids
(2) Allergy management (oral and/or nasal antihistamines , environmental
control, immunotherapy)
(3) Oxymetazoline spray: causes vasoconstriction of nasal mucosa; can
cause rebound swelling. Therefore, short use (less than 3 days) is
recommended.

b. Antibiotics
( 1 ) Cornerstone of treatment of acute sinusitis
(2) First-line: amoxicillin (or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if the patient
has a penicillin allergy) for 10 to 14 days
(3) Second-line: amoxicillin/clavulanic acid or fluoroquinolones
2. Surgical management
a. Considered an option after failure of 4 to 6 weeks of maximum medical
therapy, or for complicated acute infections
( 1 ) Complications : orbital infection, meningitis , intracranial abscess,
cavernous sinus thrombosis , invasive fungal infection (see Clinical
Pearl 1 8-2)
b . Functional endoscopic sinus surgery aims to open the natural sinus ostia
while preserving as much of the sinus mucosa as possible.

c. Surgery does not treat the underlying inflammatory causes of sinusitis , and
patients with chronic sinusitis must be managed medically following sur
gery in order to prevent recurrence.

O T O L A R Y N G O L O G Y e 3 1 9
II. Bacterial Pharyngitis
A. General principles
l . Pharyngitis : inflammation of the pharynx (most commonly the oropharynx) ,
which has many etiologies: bacterial or viral infection, carcinoma, reflux
disease, allergy, postnasal drip, environmental exposures
2. Bacterial pharyngitis accounts for 5% to 1 0% of cases of pharyngitis and
commonly affects the palatine tonsils, uvula, soft palate, and posterior
pharyngeal wall.

B. Clinical features
l . Sore throat, odynophagia, fever, chills, malaise, headache, neck stiffness , and
anorexia
2. Cervical adenopathy, pharyngeal erythema and edema, gray-white tonsillar
exudates , and petechiae on the soft palate
3. Cough is typically absent.

4. Pharyngitis can lead to peritonsillar or retropharyngeal abscess, rheumatic
fever, scarlet fever, and poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis .

C. Diagnosis
l . History and physical examination (Centor criteria) : fever, tender anterior
cervical adenopathy, tonsillar exudates, absent cough
2. Rapid antigen test (70% to 90% sensitivity, 90% to 1 00% specific)
3. Antistreptolysin titers are not helpful in diagnosis of acute pharyngitis .

D . Treatment
l . Medical management with a 1 0-day course of antibiotics
a. Penicillins (penicillin V, amoxicillin)
b. Macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin) for penicillin
allergic patients
c. Cephalosporins may be used in instances of recurrent infection refractory to
the previously mentioned therapies.

2. Surgical management: Tonsillectomy is appropriate for those patients
with recurrent bacterial pharyngitis , whose episodes do not decrease with
appropriate antibiotic therapy.

Ill. Epiglottitis
A. General principles
l . In children, median age for occurrence is 6 to 12 years old.

2. Most common pathogen associated with this disease is Haemophilus inUuenzae B
(HIB) .

3 . Incidence has decreased 90% since the introduction of the HIB vaccine.

B. Clinical features
l . Abrupt onset (within hours) and rapid progression of fever, as well as " the

## 3 Ds" : Oysphagia, Orooling, and Oistress caused by sore throat with severe

odynophagia
2. Children with this condition are often sitting up in the "tripod" or "sniffing"
position-trunk leaning forward with the neck hyperextended and chin thrust
forward-and may be reluctant to lie down.

3. Biphasic stridor is a late sign.

C. Diagnosis
l . Lateral neck X-ray: "thumbprint sign" (thickened epiglottis) . Can confirm
the diagnosis but is often unnecessary. Best for use in children in which
epiglottitis is a possibility, but another diagnosis is more likely.

2. Direct visualization should be performed in a setting in which the airway
could be secured immediately if necessary (e.g. , in the intensive care unit or
operating room, with anesthesia nearby for intubation) .

D . Treatment
l . Surgical management: Airway maintenance is the first priority. If the
patient develops respiratory arrest during examination in operating room,
endotracheal intubation or tracheotomy may be needed to maintain the
airway.

Fever, m a l a ise, h e a d a c h e ,
a n d sore t h r o a t are p re s e nt,

2. Medical management
a. Antibiotics: third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime) and an
antistaphylococcal agent active against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus
aureus (MRSA) (clindamyciAvancomycin)
b. Corticosteroids
IV. Laryngeal Trauma
A. Blunt injury
l . Most common causes include motor vehicle accidents , all-terrain vehicle
accidents, assaults, strangulation, and "clothesline" injuries.

2. The larynx is an essential component of the airway, so trauma to this structure
is life-threatening.

B. Penetrating injury
l . Most common causes include stabbings and gunshot wounds.

2. Mass and velocity determine damage to neck.

3. Often associated with damage to surrounding structures in the neck
C. Intubation injury
l . Both the act of intubation and the removal of an endotracheal tube with the
cuff inflated can cause arytenoid dislocation.

2. Prolonged intubation can cause glottic edema , ulceration, and pressure
necrosis of the larynx/trachea. This can lead to granulation tissue with
subsequent fibrosis and subglottic/tracheal stenosis in the healing process .

D . Clinical features
l . Hoarseness, anterior neck pain, dysphagia, odynophagia, and dyspnea
2. Hemoptysis, stridor, and crepitus over laryngeal cartilage
3. Damage to surrounding structures (esophagus, vessels) : hematemesis ,
subcutaneous emphysema, expanding hematoma, and bruit
E. Diagnosis
l . Flexible laryngoscopy: Check for vocal cord movement and symmetry of the
arytenoids. Can also evaluate for granulation tissue or stenosis, suggesting
intubation trauma.

## 2 . Plain films of the neck may show air around the trachea or chest that may

compromise the airway.

3. CT scan: best radiographic tool to evaluate for laryngeal injury, especially
when physical examination is normal
F. Treatment
l . Airway, breathing, and circulation (ABC)
a. Secure the airway.

b. Immobilize the cervical spine.

c. Stabilize the patient hemodynamically while controlling any bleeding.

d. Auscultate the chest because low neck injuries may involve intrathoracic
structures as well.

2. Medical management: can be used on those patients with a stable airway
a. Humidified air
b. Elevate the head of the bed.

c. Voice rest
d. Antibiotics, if there are any defects in the laryngeal mucosa
e. Serial laryngoscopic examinations
f. Steroids
3. Surgical management
a. Blunt neck trauma
( 1 ) Lacerations of the laryngeal mucosa, vocal fold immobility, exposed carti
lage, and comminuted laryngeal fractures necessitate surgical correction.

(2) Early intervention has been shown to have favorable long-term
outcome , compared to delayed treatment.

b. Penetrating neck trauma: All unstable patients (those with massive hem
orrhage, expanding hematoma, hemodynamic instability, hemothorax/
hemomediastinum, or hypovolemic shock) require immediate exploration.

O T O L A R Y N G O L O G Y e 32 1
If stable, the need for surgical intervention depends on the zone of
involvement.

( l) Zone I: sternal notch to cricoid cartilage
(a) Surgical approach to structures made difficult by nearby bony
structures
(b) Obtain angiography and esophageal studies to assess for injuries
and determine need for surgical intervention.

(2) Zone II: cricoid cartilage to angle of mandible
(a) Most frequently involved region
(b) Often explored without imaging, unless damage to another neck
zone is suspected. A select group of patients may be managed with
close observation if there are no signs of significant injury to the
maj or vessels or aerodigestive tract.

(3) Zone III: angle of mandible to skull base
(a) Also dicult to approach surgically due to bony protection
(b) Angiography is usually performed to assess need for surgery.

(c) Serial intraoral examination should be performed to assess for
development of any parapharyngeal hematomas.
